Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E250 CDI / E250d engine typically costs £4,000–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. A timing chain replacement alone costs £1,500–£2,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The OM651 (E250 CDI, 2009–2016) is a high‑tech diesel with serious drawbacks — timing chain stretch is the defining weakness, requiring a complex and costly replacement. Injector failures, oil leaks, and DPF/EGR issues are also common. The OM654 (E250d, 2016+) is lighter, more efficient, and more reliable, but turbocharger failures, injector issues, and timing chain concerns have been reported. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651 timing chain stretch and piezo injector failure (E250 CDI), OM651 oil leaks and cooling system issues, DPF and EGR problems (both generations), OM654 turbocharger failure and injector issues (E250d), and 7G‑Tronic transmission issues. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept W213 E250d (values £15,000–£30,000+), a £4,500–£5,500 reconditioned engine is 15–30% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a solid example. Against an older W212 E250 CDI (£4,000–£12,000), a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 30–80% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a good example. The Mercedes scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W212/S212/C207/A207 (2009–2016) = OM651 (2.1L, 204 bhp); W213/S213 (2016–present) = OM654 (2.0L, 194 bhp). The OM651 was subject to a recall over faulty chain tensioner sealing rings. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain, tensioner and sprocket replacement costs £1,500–£2,500. However, the OM651's timing chain is located on the output side of the engine, making replacement technically complex and costly. If the chain snaps, a full engine replacement (£4,000–£7,000) is usually required. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current E250 CDI / E250d range. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with fitted costs around £1,800–£2,800 for smaller engines — the E250's diesel engine commands a premium. A timing chain replacement alone typically costs £1,500–£2,500.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E250 CDI / E250d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM651 timing chain replacement requires removing numerous components and is technically complex. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare E250 CDI / E250d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM651 Timing Chain Stretch & Tensioner Failure (E250 CDI, 2009–2016)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or clattering noise from the engine on cold start lasting 2–3 seconds, metallic noises, engine warning light, loss of power, rough idling.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — one owner reported the rattle after 20k miles on a replacement chain
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£2,500 (timing chain, tensioner and sprockets replacement) — some specialists have quoted around £900
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,000 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM651's timing chain is the defining weakness of this engine generation. Mercedes opted for a simplex roller chain design, which proved problematic. A stretched timing chain produces a distinct rattle during engine operation and can accelerate wear on related parts. The chain is located on the output side of the engine, making replacement technically complex and costly. Mercedes issued a recall for faulty chain tensioner sealing rings that could cause oil leaks. A cold‑start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston‑valve contact and a full engine replacement. Check service history for timing chain replacement — if missing, walk away.

problems.title: OM651 Piezo Injector Failure (E250 CDI)
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, loss of power, poor fuel economy, engine misfires, ruckeln (juddering) under partial load, check engine light.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,000 (if injector failure leads to engine damage or catalytic converter failure)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or smoke occurs

problems.recommendation: Piezo injectors are the Achilles' heel of the OM651. They proved sensitive to fuel quality and operating conditions, causing erratic fuel delivery, rough running and higher consumption. In some cases, injector failure was critical and demanded expensive repairs. Injector bottom seal leaks are also common. If you experience rough idling, misfires or loss of power, have the injectors inspected immediately. Replacing a faulty injector before it fails completely can prevent damage to other engine components. Regular fuel filter changes and using premium diesel are the best prevention.

problems.title: OM651 Oil Leaks (E250 CDI)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil warning, visible oil seepage.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Widespread oil leakage caused by sealing defects is a major issue on the OM651. Faulty sealing rings on the chain tensioner were subject to a Mercedes recall. Oil leaks can contaminate components, speed up wear, and in extreme cases, oil spots could ignite if fluid reached hot elements. Regular seal replacements add notably to the cost of running an OM651‑equipped vehicle. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have the system inspected promptly. Check whether the chain tensioner recall has been completed.

problems.title: OM651 Cooling System Issues (E250 CDI)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, low coolant warning, temperature fluctuations, overheating.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: The OM651's cooling system has several weak points, including an unreliable cylinder head coolant connection that can leak. Coolant loss can lead to overheating and deform pistons and valves. This issue was addressed by a service campaign, but as with many recalls, actual completion depends on previous owners' maintenance behaviour. If you notice coolant loss or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system pressure‑tested immediately.

problems.title: DPF & EGR System Issues (Both Generations)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced power, increased fuel consumption, frequent regeneration cycles, engine management light.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles (urban‑driven cars are at highest risk)
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (DPF clean, forced regeneration, or sensor replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if DPF warning appears

problems.recommendation: DPF clogging is one of the most common issues on both the OM651 and OM654, particularly on cars driven mostly in the city. Failed EGR systems are also common. If you see a DPF warning light, take the car for a sustained motorway run at 2,000–2,500 rpm. If the light persists, have the DPF inspected. Regular motorway driving and using premium diesel can help keep the DPF healthy.

problems.title: OM654 Turbocharger & Injector Issues (E250d, 2016+)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, odd noises, excess exhaust smoke, rough running, misfires, poor acceleration
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£8,500 (if turbo failure sends debris into the engine)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if power loss or odd noises occur

problems.recommendation: The OM654 engine has its own set of issues. Premature turbocharger failures have been reported, with axial wear on the turbocharger rotor being a characteristic failure pattern. High‑pressure fuel pump failures are also reported, often related to injector issues or fuel contamination, potentially causing misfires and poor acceleration. Owners have also reported rocker arm bearing wear. If you experience power loss, odd noises, or rough running, have the vehicle inspected by a specialist.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes E250 CDI / E250d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement; chain is on output side, making it labour‑intensive
Catch it before engine damage occurs
Address seal issues promptly; recall may cover some work
Cleaning or forced regeneration is cost‑effective
Viable if caught before debris enters engine
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E250 CDI / E250d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using Mercedes‑approved oil
* OM651 (2009–2016): timing chain tensioner replaced or inspected; chain recall completed
* OM651 (2009–2016): injectors replaced or functioning correctly (no rough idle, no smoke)
* OM654 (2016+): no turbocharger issues or odd noises
* DPF health checked — regular motorway use is a good sign
* Post‑2014 models often include TSB fixes for earlier issues
* The OM651 is widely used in European taxis and can handle high mileage with proper maintenance
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these engines are unforgiving of neglect
* OM651 with cold‑start rattle — timing chain work is imminent and expensive
* Any DPF or EGR warning lights — emissions system repairs can be costly
* OM654 with turbocharger noise or power loss — expensive turbo work ahead
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes E250 CDI and E250d represent the sweet spot in the E‑Class diesel range — strong performance, excellent fuel economy, and a refined driving experience. However, the engine choice matters significantly. The OM651 (E250 CDI, 2009–2016) is a high‑tech diesel with serious drawbacks: timing chain stretch is the defining weakness, requiring a complex and costly replacement. Injector failures, oil leaks, cooling system issues, and DPF/EGR problems are also common. The OM654 (E250d, 2016+) is lighter, more efficient, and more reliable, but turbocharger failures, injector issues, and timing chain concerns have been reported. As one specialist put it: "The OM651 is a very good engine if well maintained and looked after. Regular oil changes and decent fuel will see very high mileages". Across all generations, regular maintenance is absolutely essential.

cta.label: → Compare E250 CDI / E250d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes E250 CDI or E250d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the engine (OM651 timing chain warning — critical)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM651/OM654) and confirmed oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain Work (OM651) — Any history of chain/tensioner replacement? Chain recall completed?
* Injector Health (OM651) — Any rough idle or smoke? (injector failure warning)
* Oil Leaks (OM651) — Check for oil spots under the car; sealing defects are common
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential cooling system issues
* DPF Health — Any warning lights? Ask about regeneration frequency; urban cars will have more issues
* Turbo Health (OM654) — Any whistling, power loss, or odd noises? (turbo failure warning)
* Transmission — Any jerky shifts or delays? 7G‑Tronic issues may be developing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, injector, DPF, EGR, or turbo codes are most common

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows exactly match Section 1B's Era Map — the same engine codes, years, and generation coverage.
